low.title: Gentle Anxiety Relief
steps: Try the 4-7-8 breathing technique: inhale for 4 seconds, hold for 7, exhale for 8. Repeat 3 times.
Write down what's worrying you, then ask: 'What's the worst that could happen? Can I handle it?'
5-minute mindfulness: focus on the physical sensation of your feet on the floor.
low.affirmation: Anxiety is a signal, not a verdict. You have the strength to navigate this.

high.title: Acute Anxiety First Aid
steps: 54321 grounding: name 5 things you see, 4 you can touch, 3 you hear, 2 you smell, 1 you taste.
Splash cold water on your face or hold ice for 20 seconds to activate your parasympathetic nervous system.
Text or call someone you trust — sharing the feeling reduces its intensity.
high.affirmation: This feeling will pass. You are safe right now.

low.title: Lifting Low Mood
steps: Give yourself permission to feel sad for 10 minutes — set a timer, then do one small kind act for yourself.
Listen to a song that resonates with how you feel; let the emotion move through you.
Take a 15-minute walk outside, no destination needed.
low.affirmation: Feeling sad is part of being human. Your feelings are valid and they matter.

high.title: Deep Grief Support
steps: Don't carry this alone — reach out to a friend, family member, or counsellor.
Write about what you've lost and what it meant to you; grief needs a witness.
If this heaviness has lasted more than two weeks, consider speaking with a mental health professional.
high.affirmation: Grief is love with nowhere to go. Asking for help is courage, not weakness.

low.title: Cooling Mild Frustration
steps: Count to 10 silently before responding — give your prefrontal cortex time to catch up.
Take a brisk 10-minute walk to burn off the adrenaline.
Use the 'I feel… because… I need…' framework to express what's really going on.
low.affirmation: Your anger points to something you care about. Listen to what it's telling you.

high.title: Defusing Intense Anger
steps: Leave the situation physically — distance helps you de-escalate faster than willpower alone.
Do intense exercise (push-ups, sprinting) to metabolise the stress hormones.
Wait until you're calm before addressing the issue — decisions made in anger are often regretted.
high.affirmation: You can feel angry and still choose how to respond. That gap is your power.

low.title: Everyday Stress Management
steps: List everything on your mind, then pick only your top 3 priorities for today.
Try the Pomodoro technique: 25 minutes focused work, 5-minute break.
Protect 15 minutes each day for unscheduled rest — do nothing intentionally.
low.affirmation: You don't need to solve everything at once. One step at a time is enough.

high.title: High-Stress Emergency Reset
steps: Stop everything. Take 5 slow, deep breaths — in through the nose, out through the mouth.
Ask yourself: 'Will this matter in 5 years?' — perspective is a powerful reset.
Do just one important thing today; give yourself permission to defer the rest.
high.affirmation: You've been working so hard. Rest isn't giving up — it's how you keep going.

low.title: Maintaining Emotional Balance
steps: Write down three things you're grateful for today, however small.
Do one thing purely for enjoyment today — no productivity attached.
Reach out to someone you haven't spoken to in a while.
low.affirmation: Calm is a gift. Cherish this moment of steadiness.

high.title: Sustaining Positive Momentum
steps: Reflect on what's going well — anchor your wellbeing in what's working.
Channel this good energy into something creative or meaningful.
high.affirmation: You're in a good place. Keep nurturing what brought you here.
